title: STUDY THEME,  The Humanity of Jesus. Part 29
devotional:
We learn from the resurrection of Jesus that to be truly human we need to be compassionate more than we need glory. Try and imagine for a moment, if you were Jesus, and you rose from the dead after such an unjust trial and heinous death. Surely you would want your enemies to know, surely you would want to make a significant point, by going to Jerusalem, to appear in great glory.

What did Jesus do?

He appeared to a woman who would have been considered insignificant by the world. A woman whose heart was breaking, whose world had fallen apart. This is the last time we read about Mary Magdalene in the bible. We never read of her doing any great exploits for God, she seemed to disappear. Yet in great compassion Jesus appeared to her first. She was the first person in history to encounter the resurrected Jesus.

Let us be honest, we would rather experience glory than show compassion. There is something in all of us that wants to be vindicated, we want to be proven right, and we want to prove ourselves to the world. We want to go to our Jerusalem and make a name for ourselves and humiliate our enemies. The purpose of the power of the resurrection is to explain and demonstrate God’s intention behind the cross. The power of the resurrection displays God’s love, grace and forgiveness.

Jesus in all his risen power and glory, goes to a broken hearted insignificant woman, and says ‘Mary.’ We need to grasp the truth that compassion is an expression of power and freedom and will always be necessary. Vindication and reputation may be desirable, but unlike compassion they are not a necessity for life. Compassion is feeling disturbed and distressed because of the needs of others. It is the complete opposite of living in the entrapped selfishness of a victim mentality. I love the fact that after his Resurrection Jesus did not try and restore his relationships with his disciples by trying to impress them with displays of glory and power. Instead he came close to them, gave them time, showed them patience, understood their doubts, recognised their fears, answered their questions, challenged their love and spoke peace into their hearts. Jesus allowed his disciples to experience the restoring gentleness of resurrection power.

There is a lot about the resurrection of Jesus that is unique; there are many things that we do not understand. But what we can learn from the resurrection of Jesus is that our new life in God should make us other-centred. The resurrection power of Jesus should cause a person to reach out to others; it should empower us to restore broken relationships. To live in the power of the resurrection is to live a life that is defined by love, it is to live a life that is over flowing with grace. Perhaps a lack of love for others is really an indication we have no resurrection power in our lives? Maybe the lives we have chosen to live don’t really know the power of the resurrection?

I think that living in the power of the resurrection will affirm our humanity, but it will also play havoc with our lives. We will be forgiving and restoring those who have hurt us. We will bring assurance to those who doubt us. We will be encouraging and envisioning those who failed us. If we are living lives that are empowered by the resurrection we will be living lives that are counter-cultural. We will be saying that true humanity is about love not position, about forgiveness not revenge, about relationships not displays of glory.
prayer: . Father may my life be a clear reflection of loving resurrected power.
html:
STUDY THEME, The Humanity of Jesus. Part 29

We learn from the resurrection of Jesus that to be truly human we need to be compassionate more than we need glory. Try and imagine for a moment, if you were Jesus, and you rose from the dead after such an unjust trial and heinous death. Surely you would want your enemies to know, surely you would want to make a significant point, by going to Jerusalem, to appear in great glory.

What did Jesus do?

He appeared to a woman who would have been considered insignificant by the world. A woman whose heart was breaking, whose world had fallen apart. This is the last time we read about Mary Magdalene in the bible. We never read of her doing any great exploits for God, she seemed to disappear. Yet in great compassion Jesus appeared to her first. She was the first person in history to encounter the resurrected Jesus.

Let us be honest, we would rather experience glory than show compassion. There is something in all of us that wants to be vindicated, we want to be proven right, and we want to prove ourselves to the world. We want to go to our Jerusalem and make a name for ourselves and humiliate our enemies. The purpose of the power of the resurrection is to explain and demonstrate God’s intention behind the cross. The power of the resurrection displays God’s love, grace and forgiveness.

Jesus in all his risen power and glory, goes to a broken hearted insignificant woman, and says ‘Mary.’ We need to grasp the truth that compassion is an expression of power and freedom and will always be necessary. Vindication and reputation may be desirable, but unlike compassion they are not a necessity for life. Compassion is feeling disturbed and distressed because of the needs of others. Instead he came close to them, gave them time, showed them patience, understood their doubts, recognised their fears, answered their questions, challenged their love and spoke peace into their hearts. There is a lot about the resurrection of Jesus that is unique; there are many things that we do not understand. The resurrection power of Jesus should cause a person to reach out to others; it should empower us to restore broken relationships. Perhaps a lack of love for others is really an indication we have no resurrection power in our lives? Maybe the lives we have chosen to live don’t really know the power of the resurrection?

I think that living in the power of the resurrection will affirm our humanity, but it will also play havoc with our lives. We will be forgiving and restoring those who have hurt us. We will bring assurance to those who doubt us. We will be encouraging and envisioning those who failed us. We will be saying that true humanity is about love not position, about forgiveness not revenge, about relationships not displays of glory.

Prayer: . Father may my life be a clear reflection of loving resurrected power.
Bible in a year reading plan: Joshua 1:10-4:24
